speaker: interviewer
text: --yeah it's-- yeah, I'm recording the microphone now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, you're recording the microphone. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ask her about Florida.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, I-- I ask you about Florida. What about Florida? What do you like about Florida? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well-- [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: It's hot.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's hot.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you like about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: There's things about Florida that I like and things that I don't.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what do you like?

speaker: interviewee
text: The things that I like is I can wear shorts all the time-- [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --and comfortable clothes. That's why you're not wearing shorts today.

speaker: interviewee
text: Comfortable clothes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How are we doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- am I on or what?

speaker: interviewer
text: You're on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, talk.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's not recording. You're just testing--

speaker: interviewee
text: Florida is-- if I wouldn't be allergic to the sun and bug bites, then Florida is a excellent place.

speaker: interviewer
text: You and my wife-- the bugs and the sun.

speaker: interviewee
text: The bugs and the sun.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I have to be protected.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's good? Yeah. Let's start.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I'm allergic to the sun.

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't really care.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it's a nice life in Florida. It's--

speaker: interviewer
text: No complaints-- how long you been here now?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll be nine years in September.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're an old-timer.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I-- I enjoyed when my husband worked, because it was a normal life. He went to work, and I did my thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today is May 25, 1995. I'm interviewing the survivor, Lola Kay. I am Chaim Rosov. We are in Coconut Creek, Florida. And the language to be spoken will be English-- tape number one. Today is May 25, 1995. I am interviewing Lola Kay. I am Chaim Rosov. We are in Coconut Creek, Florida. And the interview will be conducted in English.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good morning, Lola. Good morning, Chaim.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to begin with you telling me something about where you were born and when you were born.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Lublin, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: September the 23rd, 1925.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your family-- tell me about your mother, your father, your--

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother and my father-- we were sort of religious-- not-- not real Hasidic--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --but Orthodox--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --house. I was brought up. I went to a Bais Yaakov. And I had-- we were four sisters and three brothers. I was the youngest. Most of them were married when I, you know, grew up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they had children on their own.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they weren't living with you at that time. They had their own families.

speaker: interviewee
text: They weren't living in the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was in your home when you were growing up? Your mother, your father, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother, and my father, and one sister. She survived. Her name is Gitel. And she lives in the States on Staten Island.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the schooling you had as a-- as a young girl.

speaker: interviewee
text: As a young girl, it was difficult for me to go to a public school, because of my name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rabinowitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the children, you know, the gentile children, they were very cruel. They call you names, things like this. In the beginning, I had gentile friends, because of the neighbors--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --very close friends. And-- but then, when it starts getting worse, near the, you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: Before 1939.

speaker: interviewee
text: Before 1939. So we had to, even in Bais Yaakov, we had to go to school secretly, you know. The school--

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say secretly, how do you go to school secretly? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: How you go secretly, because you should go to a public school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because it was mandatory to go to a school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that, according to the Polish or whatever-- that didn't count, you know, a religious school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was a religious school. And then I-- I was in the-- enrolled in the-- my father was a Zionist. It was always that [NON-ENGLISH]. It's got to be full, and you take it. And on Friday nights, he had friends in with tea. And they always discussed Israel. His whole life was Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you-- you led, basically, a normal life--

speaker: interviewee
text: A normal life--

speaker: interviewer
text: --until?

speaker: interviewee
text: --until they started-- there were programs here and there. If any--

speaker: interviewer
text: Before 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Before-- if anything happened, like a-- one Jewish boy and a Gentile boy-- he was, you know, a high in the government, a son of one of the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --[NON-ENGLISH]. And he-- he was trying to be a macho guy, this Jewish guy and this gentile. For a while, they would start to fight, you know, on the street. And then one morning we got up, and he was killed-- that gentile guy.

speaker: interviewer
text: The gentile boy was killed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Was killed by the Jewish boy. And then it was a black-- what was it-- Friday. Every home was, you know, locked.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there were pogroms and things. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was responsible for the programs? Who-- who was--

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the anti-Semites, the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Ordinary people living in the town.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ordinary people, you know, on a Sunday.

speaker: interviewer
text: It wasn't organized, was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was like-- like today, they have things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Gangs.

speaker: interviewee
text: It takes one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, and they ganged up. And they broke windows. And on Friday night, you had to shut the-- the, you know, the drapes. And you light your candles. And that's how you live.

speaker: interviewer
text: In other words, you didn't want the outside to see.

speaker: interviewee
text: The outside that you light candles--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --on a Passover, the-- the-- that the Jews make matzah from gentile blood. They need gentile blood.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went through this for-- for a number of years-- the anti-Semitism.

speaker: interviewee
text: The anti-Semitism--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was constant.

speaker: interviewee
text: --constantly. So that-- that was our life-- constant fear. You live in fear. And then it was in 1939. So we had to put up with what was going on in Germany. 1940-- [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --they try to get the Jews out of Germany, you know-- Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --into Poland-- the German Jews--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --resettle them. They throw us out from our homes. They were concentrated in one-- in a room. And they took everything. And they gave to the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were forced out of your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Forced out.

speaker: interviewer
text: You left everything there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And I got-- What did you take with you? Did you take anything with you? What did they allow you to take with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you-- you only took-- [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --whatever the necessity to-- Clothing?

speaker: interviewee
text: --to-- to live.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anything else-- you had good dishes or good-- good furniture, everything good, you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: Stayed there.

speaker: interviewee
text: --was-- stayed there. And it got so bad. And the-- you know. And you weren't allowed to do any-- anything to make any money, to survive on. And food was scarce. Even if you had the money, you couldn't get to it to buy anything. Because the-- the Jews, they had stores. They broke the windows. They, you know, robbed them. And it was very difficult. I didn't understood, because then I had no idea what was going on. But my father knew that it's so bad. He felt probably sorry that he had a chance to go to Israel and-- you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: He should've left.

speaker: interviewee
text: He should have left. But-- and his-- you know, you-- you have to leave family. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: It's hard.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because he wore-- he had a beard, you know, a small like Theodor Herzl.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know that picture?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Like, you know-- he-- he kept it nice, groomed and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father had a business.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, what did he-- what did he do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he had storage of all kind of grains-- wheat. They used to bring the peasants--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --you know, from the farmers and the storage. And then they used to ship all that wheat to-- I don't know, Germany, whatever, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So up to a point, you had your own food, because of-- of his work.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We had food. But then later, that everything was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --confiscated, because of the-- it got so bad that my sister-- is the one she was married-- she lived at the outskirts of Lublin, called-- near Lublin-- Glusk. It was like beautiful place. And they had meals, you see?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: All that meal-- flour-- the best flour, I remember-- they had all those meals. And they were confiscated also.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when the-- when the Germans moved in, they took the family. The ones they were well off-- they picked on the first ones they were well off.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my brother-in-law's-- brother-in-law and my father-- and the-- they took him out. And they beat him up-- the Germans-- so bad that he spit up blood. And then-- then that-- he recovered a little. They took him back and forth, just because they had all those things that a Jew-- why should you have anything? Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they died.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean? They beat them to death?

speaker: interviewee
text: They beat them to death. They didn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: That was your sister's husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was my sister's brother-- my-- my brother-in-law's brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your brother-in-law's brother, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Brother-- and a whole bunch of other people. You know, they were related. They were in the mills, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a big-- it was called Cross's Mill, the best flour in-- in probably in Poland. Then it became so bad that I couldn't do anything, that I wanted to, like play with kids, or go out, or what. We belonged-- I belonged to the chalutzim as a child. And we didn't had any meetings. My-- my parents wouldn't move, because it was too late. Then, even if you wanna get-- we didn't had anybody in the United States to have a sponsor--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --to go to or what. The only place was where you were born, and that-- our family.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in 1942, they began to be that they gonna liquidate all the Jews, all the ghettos. And they're gonna make clean from Jews, of course, judenrein. I happened to be in the-- with the-- listening in where they-- they had a conference in the judenrein. And I listened in.

speaker: interviewee
text: They said, well, the girls have a chance to go to Germany on Aryan papers, you know, to hide as Aryans. But for the boys, I'm afraid there is no chance.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's a dead giveaway that they're Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the brothers, they had wives. And they went to the partisans, to join the partisans. So-- Do you know what happened to them with the partisans, because I know the--

speaker: interviewee
text: They were killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: The partisans did not care for the Jews being part of them, I remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't-- they didn't want to accept them.

speaker: interviewer
text: They didn't accept them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one of them that I adored-- his name was [NON-ENGLISH]. He was, you know-- I-- I really was so attached to him. And he went, and he was killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: By the partisans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not by the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: By the partisans. And one went to Majdanek. And he escaped from Majdanek last time. I was told by my sister, because she kept in touch. She was much older than I was. So he escaped, and he was killed too.

speaker: interviewer
text: What, they recaptured him? Or he--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. They were killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were just killed.

speaker: interviewee
text: 'Cause the fight.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And all three were killed. Whoever went, you know, into the forest to the partisans--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --they were killed. And to the end, we went-- there is no place where to hide. It was, you know, by Friday, they said they're gonna have to-- everybody's got to leave. Not one Jew is gonna remain in the city. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were with your sister at this point.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was the only-- my-- my parents--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --they had a bunker--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --with a-- with, you know, friends of my father. They figured the war was gonna only last maybe two more weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they're gonna survive. They apparently had enough food, things what they needed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they built a bunker in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Underground, a bunker.

speaker: interviewer
text: They built a bunker.

speaker: interviewee
text: Bunker.

speaker: interviewer
text: In-- in the home? In the basement of the home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And the-- [SIGHS] --they had everything in there. And so we said goodbye. My mother had her earrings-- she took them off. She said to my sister-- I remember that was on a Friday-- you take them. This is gonna probably help you to survive when you need, you know, to buy your time. She said, I can't take them. You keep them in your ears the way you always wore them. And I was shivering. I came up with the idea, actually, to go in on Aryan papers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I hear-- overheard what they said in the, you know, in--

speaker: interviewer
text: About the Judenrein.

speaker: interviewee
text: --about the Judenrein.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was either that or go-- go in on that [NON-ENGLISH] place, whatever they called-- everybody's gotta be concentrated in one place. Whoever is a Jew has gotta be in one place, concentrated. And then, you know, not one Jew has gotta be in the city anymore. So we started out. It was like daybreak. And my mother said goodbye. And she said to my sister, her last word.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- [CRYING] --she said, take care. She should take care on me. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother stayed? Your mother stayed in the bunker? And you and your sister left.

speaker: interviewee
text: She didn't leave. I weren't in the bunker yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, she didn't go to the bunker yet.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we said goodbye.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: After we left. We left. We started walking. [SIGHS] And we walked-- I don't know how long, maybe a 1/2 a day. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And then-- Well, I'm curious. I'm curious. Did your sister finally take the earrings or not?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother kept the earrings, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: She kept the earrings. She said, don't.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we kept walking. And then my sister remind herself, there is somebody who is doing something, you know, hiding you out. She knew a woman.

speaker: interviewee
text: This woman, she takes money for it. And she hides you in-- because if it's only two weeks, you know, we can survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: We walked in. She said, OK. And she took our things, what we had with us. And she put them in-- there was-- she had one room in a barn where the cows--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --you know, next to the cows. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then she said, you can stay here. That was on a Friday. Saturday-- Sunday she went to church. She said, she's going to church.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my sister mentioned that my brother was supposed to, you know, hide out. The mayor from the city-- Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --was friendly with him. I don't know what-- maybe he did him favors before-- that he's gonna hide him out. And she told her where to go. She gave her our address. She wrote down on a paper where to go and ask if he is still there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: She left, and she came back. It was already like nighttime. And she said, your brother is not there anymore. And OK. That was Sunday morning. We stayed there. Sunday-- no. That was Sunday night already.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: About-- we went to sleep. It was some-- on the floor. We slept on the floor with bugs and everything. And about 12 o'clock, after 12:00, we heard a knock at the door. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And she went. She opened the door. And three men walked in in, you know, German uniforms. And the minute they walked in, we were frightened. We didn't know. And then she-- they recognized us. They recognized my sister. And they didn't said anything. They-- they just looked. And my--

speaker: interviewer
text: They recognized your sister, because they were from Lublin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my sister said, we can pay you to leave us alone. We had money.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] The first thing was said-- she took our things. We had nothing, you know, with us. She took all her things, and she put on the-- in the barn.

speaker: interviewer
text: She hide our things. How can we get some things back? And then he told her to go and bring the things back. By the time he told her to bring things back, he told us, run. Don't look back. He opened the door. As fast as you can-- don't ever look back. So did they take money from you, or they just let you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: She gave them--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, they took the money.

speaker: interviewee
text: She gave them money.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: But never mind the money.

speaker: interviewer
text: I thought out of the goodness of their heart--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --they let you go.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they opened the door. Don't ever look back. Just keep going-- running-- not going, but run. And we walked out. Like this--

speaker: interviewer
text: That was the first miracle. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: First miracle-- that's true. So we walked. It was all night. We didn't know which way we walked. Apparently, it was-- it led us to a big forest. They call them Black Forest. We walked in this-- that forest. We breathed a little easier, because there were no people. We're gonna be, you know, only us and that-- those-- that forest. But we walked into deep. It was so dark that we haven't seen any sky. And then we heard-- we walked a couple of hours. We heard crying-- a baby crying so loud and woman's-- a woman's voice. And we followed that, you know, sound from where it came.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my sister walked in, and my sister recognized. She knew the woman. She had about a nine-month-old baby in her arms.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she dried up. She hadn't fed the baby for a couple of days maybe. She said she was tearing her hair out. And she was screaming. My sister said to her, you want to join us? She said, to leave the baby? No, I want to die with the baby. I can never do that-- leave the baby. And we couldn't, you know, stay there, because she was screaming.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were afraid that they-- somebody is gonna hear. And we started to walk. We walked-- it was already noon. And there was no end to this forest. Finally, we saw a little light. And then we saw a path going. And we walked through that path to lead us someplace. When we walked out, it was a field of, you know, officers training on horses. So my sister was panic. She thought they-- they come and-- to-- to-- to, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, they saw you.

speaker: interviewee
text: That they saw me-- I don't even-- I don't know if they saw us. But she panicked.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she said, you wanted to leave. Because I was afraid, you know. So she said, you wanted to leave. She just throw me down. And she said, you wanted to leave. And she grabbed me, you know. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: She was angry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Angry at me that I wanted to leave-- why she didn't went with the parents or-- or--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, she--

speaker: interviewee
text: --or went to, you know, the-- on that to Kowel, to the concentration camp back, you know, like everybody else did. And then, they-- eventually they turned around-- those-- those officers. They had enough training. They just turned around, and they went their way. Then--

speaker: interviewer
text: They saw you, and they-- and they circled you, and then they left you.

speaker: interviewee
text: The didn't saw me. That was about quite a distance.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: But you saw them. It was quite--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, you saw them

speaker: interviewee
text: --a distance.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And even if they saw, they probably wouldn't know who it is--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they left.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we always had in that mind, you know, that everybody who is gonna see you--

speaker: interviewer
text: Will kill you.

speaker: interviewee
text: --will kill-- will kill you because you're Jewish. So then it was easier on-- on her. I said, OK. I brushed off. And we started again. We started walking. And there was no end to that walk. I was just field.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was already going to get dark. And we didn't know where-- we are going to wind up in another forest? Finally, we turned-- we turned on another, you know, path we saw. And we started to this, but where it's gonna lead us? So we saw smoke coming out of, you know, smoke. Then it must be a house someplace. If there's smoke, there's a house.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we walked towards this house-- nearer. [SIGHS] We walked. So we walked in. We didn't care what's gonna be anymore. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So we walked in, and he's-- he probably knew. This man knew something. Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, we can stay in the barn. Then he said to her, I have a bunker where they keep potatoes for the winter, and what. And he's gonna keep her there. Because he doesn't have any children. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So he's gonna, you know, after nine months-- until nine months. He wanted to have a baby with your sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: With my-- my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the meantime, he said, you can stay on-- on the-- in the-- on that-- in the barn--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: --where the cows. And you walked up on a ladder. And there was straw there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we walked up. We rested. And then my sister said to me-- she didn't want to tell me what he told her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that-- [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --maybe I wouldn't understand then. Right.
